artifacts.content:
Laura,

I’m still at my desk trying to tighten up the council packet for the F‑250, but my head’s all over the place, so I’m just going to brain‑dump to you for a minute.

When I was drafting the background earlier, I caught myself almost typing out the whole January 14 saga – starting the day at St. Joseph’s with Dad and Dr. Carlson messing with his heart failure meds, then getting called out that night to the Oak Street water main break by Essentia–Brainerd Clinic, bouncing around in Unit 23 while the transmission slipped twice on the way back. It’s exactly why I’m pushing this replacement, but I can’t drag Dad’s cardiology stuff into a public narrative.

Same thing with last winter. Every time I look at that truck I think about the Feb 8, 2025 storm when Mark broke down on Co Rd 3 by the high school and I had to walk out of Ethan’s session with Dr. Schultz at Brainerd Lakes Counseling. His anxiety went through the roof when I said I might have to go back out in the blizzard. I’ll talk to Council about breakdowns and overtime, not about counseling and panic attacks.

Honestly, all of it is tangled up with my own heart junk too. We’re still chipping away at those Essentia angioplasty bills from March 2023, and I keep thinking if this truck comes in at $54,973.26 like the quote, at least I’m not giving Mark any excuse to hammer me at the November 2026 review.

Anyway, kids home yet from practice? If you throw something in the slow cooker, I’ll try to be out of here by 6:30.

– M

Personal notes – Unit 23 / life stuff

• Remember why this replacement matters beyond the numbers.

1) February 8, 2025 storm – Mark broke down with Unit 23 on County Road 3 heading toward the downed tree by Brainerd High. I was sitting in the tiny room at Brainerd Lakes Counseling Center with Ethan and Dr. Karen Schultz for his anxiety session. Phone kept buzzing about the breakdown and rerouting. Ethan’s hands were shaking when I stepped out to take the call. I cannot keep putting him in that spot.

2) December 19, 2025 ice on Riverside Drive – headlights on 23 cut out again while plowing near Gregory Park. Had to call Daniel at the Brainerd Alano Club and ask him to leave his AA meeting early to watch Grace at Pine Ridge so I could go deal with it. He was gracious, but that’s not fair to his recovery.

3) April 3, 2025 – hauling barricades to the water main break by Crow Wing County Jail when 23 coughed and died again. Missed the virtual follow‑up with Megan Lewis from Northwest Management about the March written warning on my take‑home fuel use. I keep telling myself I’m “demonstrating better fleet management,” but I need to actually do it.

Talking points for myself: focus on mileage, $6,800 repairs in 18 months, safety, overtime. Do NOT drag Ethan’s counseling, Daniel’s AA, or HR warnings into anything public.

metadata.title: Why I have to get Unit 23 replaced

artifacts.content:
Karen,

Long one, sorry, but you’re the only one who really gets the overlap between family stuff and this job.

I’m polishing the staff memo on the F‑250 and trying not to think about all the personal landmines tied to that stupid truck. When it died on East River Road during the Feb 9–10, 2025 storm, I was literally sitting in a disciplinary meeting at Brainerd High about Ethan’s two‑day suspension for vaping in the parking lot. Mark had to pull a truck off the southeast route to tow 23 back to the garage, and residents started calling about unplowed streets. I can’t write “missed calls because I was at my kid’s suspension meeting” in any official document, but that’s what happened.

Same with the financial side. I keep telling Finance that the $54,973.26 price is under the $60k allocation in Fund 402 and that there’s a $5,026.74 cushion for wiring and plow controls. What I don’t say is that at home we’re still paying off about $18,400 from my March 2023 emergency angioplasty at Essentia–St. Joe’s, plus Ethan’s $3,200 restitution from the BHS vandalism last November and Grace’s $1,800 orthodontics plan at Lakes Area Dental that started January 2025. I cannot afford any hint of “sloppy with money” in front of Mark before my November 2026 review.

On top of that, Mom and Dad being at Good Samaritan Bethany since August 2024 means I’m covering roughly $600 a month for their assisted living. It’s not their fault, but it all piles up. One bad storm, one preventable accident with this failing truck, and we’re talking about cashing out retirement or losing the house.

Thanks for letting me unload.

Mike

metadata.subject: Why I’m so wound up about this truck

– Last time we were here, storm rolled in and Unit 23 broke down on County Road 3 near Brainerd High while Mark was trying to get to a downed tree. I had to leave early to coordinate towing and coverage; Ethan’s anxiety spiked.

– Remember: later, when the F‑250 quote comes in ~$5,026.74 under the $60,000 budget, use that cushion to avoid extra OT this winter. Don’t give Steve or HR any more ammo.

– Follow‑up after mild heart attack Nov 2025.
– Would like to walk in saying the $54,973.26 F‑250 purchase and surplus sale of Unit 23 are done so I can tell Laura the extra hours meant something concrete.

metadata.title: Key appointments – Ethan, HR, and my heart

artifacts.content:
Dan,

I owe you an explanation for why I’ve been so intense about this stupid 3/4‑ton replacement.

You remember the December 19, 2025 ice event on Riverside Drive. Unit 23’s headlights cut out again while it was plowing near Gregory Park. I called you at the Brainerd Alano Club and asked you to duck out of your AA meeting early so you could come over to Pine Ridge and sit with Grace. You didn’t hesitate, but I’ve felt guilty ever since. That truck should not be the reason you miss recovery time.

It’s not the first time family stuff and that truck have collided. During the Feb 9–10, 2025 storm it died on East River Road while I was locked in that disciplinary meeting at Brainerd High over Ethan’s two‑day suspension for vaping in the parking lot. Mark had to yank another truck off its route to tow 23 back, and half the southeast side was late getting cleared.

Money is part of it too. Laura and I are still digging out from my March 2023 emergency angioplasty at Essentia–St. Joe’s – about $18,400 left. Then there’s the $9,750 we pulled from the Bremer HELOC in October 2024 to help you with the DUI fines and Northland Recovery. I don’t resent it, but it means I can’t afford to look reckless at work. The only good news is the new F‑250 came in at $54,973.26, under the $60,000 budget in Fund 402, so at least I can show I’m not blowing city money.

Anyway, thanks again for bailing me out that night at the Alano Club. I’m trying to make sure we’re never in that position again.

Mike

metadata.subject: Why I keep talking about that damn truck

artifacts.content:
Private journal – health, work, and the truck

October 10, 2025

Back home from Cuyuna Regional Medical Center after the Oct 3–7 stress‑related hospitalization. Dr. Harris in occupational medicine didn’t mince words: the overnight call‑outs and constant equipment failures (read: Unit 23) are pushing my blood pressure into the danger zone. I promised Laura, Mom, and Dad I’d cut 10–15 on‑call hours a month.

The problem is, this job doesn’t slow down just because my heart or head says so. On November 9, 2025, the 3/4‑ton died again near South 6th while I was on a sanding route. I left the truck, got a ride back so I could make the video call with the social worker at University of Minnesota Masonic Children’s Hospital about Grace’s anxiety plan. Tried to act calm on camera while thinking about unsanded intersections.

Then there’s Ethan. The August 2025 marijuana possession case, the court‑ordered outpatient at Essentia–Brainerd Clinic, the nights the truck stalls on Riverside and I end up hopping into his car to make his treatment slot on time. I’m supposed to be present as a dad and present as Public Works Director, and this failing pickup keeps forcing impossible choices.

Laura and I met with Karen Schultz at Bremer Bank on September 22, 2025. If I lose this job over a preventable safety incident with that truck, we’d probably have to cash out about $35,000 in retirement just to keep the Pine Ridge mortgage and Ethan’s $420/month car payment afloat. That’s the backdrop when I push so hard to get the replacement through, strictly within the Equipment Replacement Fund.

Mom,

I wanted to catch you up on a few things before I swing by Good Samaritan Bethany on Sunday.

First, the good news: the replacement F‑250 for Streets is finally moving. Lakeside Ford’s quote is $54,973.26, all within Fund 402, no extra hit to the General Fund. After everything we’re juggling financially – your and Dad’s assisted living (my share is about $600 a month), Ethan’s messes, Grace’s braces – it’s a relief to have at least one big number that’s clean and fully budgeted.

I keep thinking back to March 2025 when Dad was at Essentia–St. Joseph’s for heart surgery and we were talking about maybe contracting out the northeast quadrant plow routes to Northstar Snow Services so I could be at the hospital more and rest Unit 23. We never pulled the trigger, but that was the first time I really admitted to myself that the fleet – and my schedule – weren’t sustainable.

Since then it’s just piled on: the written reprimand in February 2025 over how I handled the salt storage contract, missing a follow‑up with Megan Lewis from Northwest Management after Unit 23 died by the jail on April 3, the December 27–29, 2024 storm where two operators logged 18 hours of OT in three days because that truck was down again. One of them told me in the garage his wife might leave if he keeps missing supervised visits with their 6‑year‑old at the Crow Wing County Family Visitation Center.

I know you and Dad worry that I’m overdoing it. I’m trying to show Karen (in her City Administrator hat) that I can clean this up before my June 2026 review and still be there for you two. Getting this truck replaced the right way is part of that.

Love,

Mike
